coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots assembled on layered Ti3C2Tx exhibit excellent tribological properties
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ding
Jia, Xiaohua
Zhang, Yiping
Shan, Zhiqiang
Yang, Jin
Su, Yunfeng
Song, Haojie
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) as a lubricant additive, is limited by its single tribological mechanisms and difficult to surface functionalization. Therefore, it will be anticipated to combine NCQDs with two-dimensional layered materials, so as to improve lubrication and interface bonding performance. Due to the active groups existing on the surface of Ti3C2Tx, the NCQDs is assembled on the two-dimensional material Ti3C2Tx, thus the NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx composite can be used as a favorable lubricant additive. The NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx composite can exert synergistic lubrication and chemical reactions during the friction process in comparison to NCQDs as lubricant additives, resulting in forming a friction transfer film between the surface. Therefore, this study confirms that NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx composite exhibits enormous potential as a lubricant additive.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Ti3C2Tx
Synergistic lubrication
Friction chemical reaction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthetic processes of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx.
(a) SEM image of Ti3AlC2. (b) SEM images of Ti3C2Tx. (c) TEM images of Ti3C2Tx. (d) TEM images of NCQDs. (e) (f) TEM images of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx composites.
(a) XRD patterns of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx. (b) Raman spectra of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx. (c) FT-IR spectra of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx.

(a) Friction curve and (d) average friction coefficient and wear track width at 0.01–0.125 wt% concentration. (b) friction curves and (e) average friction coefficient and wear track width under different loads (1–3 N). (c) friction curve and (f) average friction coefficient and wear track width at 100–400 rpm.

(a) Friction curve and (b) average friction coefficient and wear track width at 0.01–0.125 wt% concentration. (d) friction curves and (e) average friction coefficient and wear track width under different loads (0.5–2.5 N). (c) friction curve and (f) average friction coefficient and wear track width at 100–400 rpm.

The 0.05 wt% NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx SFO dispersion worked for a long time (a) friction curve, (b) wear track width and (c) morphology. 0.075 wt% NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx PAO10 dispersion under long time working (a) Friction curve (d) Wear track width and (e) Morphology.

SFO, 0.05 wt% NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx, PAO10 and 0.075 wt% NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx wear surface morphology. (a-d) optical microscope images. (e-h) three-dimensional morphology after optical microscope photo processing. (i-l) SEM images.

XPS spectra of worn surfaces of 0.05 wt% NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx SFO dispersions: (a) N 1 s fine spectra, (b) O 1 s fine spectra and (c) Ti 2p fine spectra. XPS spectra of worn surfaces of 0.075 wt% NCQDs@ Ti3C2Tx PAO10 dispersions: (d) N 1 s fine spectra, (e) O 1 s fine spectra and (f) Ti 2p fine spectra.
(a) FT-IR spectra and (b) (c) Raman spectra of the worn surfaces of 0.05 wt% NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx SFO dispersion and 0.075 wt% NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx PAO10 dispersion.
(a) friction diagram; (b) schematic diagram of contact area between steel ball and steel plate. (c) Shear effect and rolling bearing effect. (d) Filling effect.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots assembled on layered Ti3C2Tx exhibit excellent tribological properties

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Supervision

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Green Preparation and Functionalization for Inorganic Materials, Shaanxi University of Science & Technology, Xi’an 710021, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Green Preparation and Functionalization for Inorganic Materials, Shaanxi University of Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Green Preparation and Functionalization for Inorganic Materials, Shaanxi University of Science & Technology, Xi’an 710021, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) as a lubricant additive, is limited by its single tribological mechanisms and difficult to surface functionalization. Therefore, it will be anticipated to combine NCQDs with two-dimensional layered materials, so as to improve lubrication and interface bonding performance. Due to the active groups existing on the surface of Ti3C2Tx, the NCQDs is assembled on the two-dimensional material Ti3C2Tx, thus the NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx composite can be used as a favorable lubricant additive. The NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx composite can exert synergistic lubrication and chemical reactions during the friction process in comparison to NCQDs as lubricant additives, resulting in forming a friction transfer film between the surface. Therefore, this study confirms that NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx composite exhibits enormous potential as a lubricant additive.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a novel carbon-based nanomaterial, possesses many unique properties such as unique zero-dimensional structure, controllable functional surface, small size effect and good biocompatibility, which have been used as lubricant additives [1–3]. Because CQDs have the advantages of controllable functional surface, the CQDS can be doped with nitrogen, thus promoting their further application [4,5]. Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs-N) was prepared using pyrolysis method by Ye et al. [6] The rich functional groups containing oxygen and nitrogen on the surface of CQDs-N enable it to be easily and quickly adsorbed on the metal surface, playing a rolling and protective role. Cui et al. [7] composited nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) using hydrothermal method. In order to reduce metal corrosion, a nitride protective film was formed by utilizing the bonding between the metal surface and nitrogen. These significant improvements are mainly attributed to the fact that nitrogen, an active atom, can easily form coordination bonds by contacting with metal atoms and exhibit strong appetency on the metal surface, so as to facilitate the formation of compounds and protective film between metal surfaces [8].

However, in order to meet the constantly updated industrial requirements, the tribological properties of NCQDs lubricant materials still need to be continuously improved. At present, improving the lubrication performance through combining several materials has become a powerful means. The N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) can modify 2D Ni-BDC nanosheets using in-situ solvothermal method to synthesize N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites. The tribological properties of N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites under different friction pairs in castor oil were deeply studied [9]. The results showed that the dispersity of N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC nanocomposites were observably improved, and the friction coefficient and wear track diameter with 0.04 wt% N-CDs/2D Ni-BDC dispersed in castor oil under ball-ball contact were 35.29% and 25% lower than those of 2D Ni-BDC, respectively. The friction coefficient and wear rate under ball-slider contact decreased by 20% and 12.3%, respectively. A new lubricating additive of N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) nanoparticles was prepared using solvothermal method. The tribological performance of N-CQDs added to MoS2 nanofluids was evaluated [10]. Compared with MoS2 nanofluids without N-CQD, MoS2 nanofluids with 0.4 wt% N-CQD can reduce the friction coefficient and wear rate by 30.4% and 31.0% respectively. Through the analysis of the wear surface morphology and chemical composition, the friction film induced by friction chemical reactions exhibited excellent lubrication performance. Therefore, by combining with other materials, nitrogen-doped quantum dots (NCQDs) significantly improve the performance of lubricating materials in dispersion, lubrication and interface bonding. The tribological properties of the synthesized composites are markedly better than those of single materials.

Surprisingly, MXene as a new kind of two-dimensional transition metal carbon/nitride nanomaterial, has numerous advantages such as graphite-like multilayer structure, low shear strength, self-lubricating ability, large surface area and rich active sites, making it a rising star in lubricating materials [11–15]. Gao et al. [16] functionalized Ti3C2Tx with dialkyl dithiophosphate, which improved the friction-reduction and anti-wear ability of Ti3C2Tx in 500SN base oil. Lian et al. [17] evaluated the tribological properties of Ti3C2 as a solid lubricant, and the friction coefficient of the modified Ti3C2 coating was reduced significantly. Liu et al. [18] prepared Ti3C2Tx nanosheets by HF etching method, achieving the maximum reduction of friction and wear at the optimal concentration. MXene enjoying weak van der Waals force between layers can easily slide relatively under shear force, and due to the existence of rich functional groups on the surface, it can be easily functionalized to meet the expected performance under various friction environments [19,20]. In addition, these studies demonstrated that MXene lubricating materials can be easily modified or functionalized, and exhibit excellent lubrication performance, which provides favorable conditions for the combination between MXene lubricating materials and other materials.

Therefore, the lubricity and interfacial bonding ability of single materials can be improved by combining NCQDs with two-dimensional materials, so as to exhibit synergistic lubrication of zero-dimensional and two-dimensional materials [9,10]. It is anticipated to realize the combination between NCQDs and Ti3C2Tx through the interaction among functional groups on the surface, which can further enhance the friction-reduction and anti-wear properties of single materials.

Herein, the NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx composite was successfully synthesized by mechanically mixing, followed by stirring for a long time, and then NCQDs was assembled on Ti3C2Tx layered structure by coprecipitation method. The interaction among functional groups can promote the forming process of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx composite. The synergistic lubrication effect generated by combining NCQDs lubricant additive with Ti3C2Tx can facilitate the formation of friction transfer film in the surface, which is beneficial to realize excellent friction-reduction and anti-wear properties. The tribological mechanism was discussed by analyzing the chemical reactions of friction surface and calculating the lubrication regime. This study demonstrated that NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx composite will be a promising substitute for oil-based lubricating additives.

Sodium hydroxide particles and acetaldehyde (40% aqueous solution) were bought from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Ti3AlC2 was obtained from Shanghai Xiyan New Material Technology Co., Ltd. Anhydrous ethanol, urea (CH₄N₂O), hydrochloric acid and hydrofluoric acid (HF) were supplied from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Poly-alphaolefin (PAO10) was obtained from Tiancheng Meijia Lubricating Oil Co., Ltd. Sunflower oil (SFO) was supplied by Yihai Kerry Arawana Holdings Co. Ltd. All reagents are analytically pure and could be used without further purification.

NCQDs lubricating additives were prepared by aldol condensation reaction. Unsaturated aldehydes produced in the reaction can experience a chain of reactions. When CH2N2O is added, nitrogen atoms can be introduced by the interaction between aldehyde groups and amino. Various polymer chains are produced in this series of complex reactions, and these intertwined and crosslinked polymer chains are carbonized to form carbon cores. Therefore, NCQDs are made up of a carbon core and a structure of polymer/carbon hybrid, enjoying rich functional groups/polymer chains on the surface, being an inadequate carbonization state.

The detailed synthetic process is as follows. NCQDs were synthesized using aldol condensation reaction. Urea (2.0 g) and sodium hydroxide (6.0 g) were slowly added while stirring in 20 mL acetaldehyde. After 2 h of stirring, the product was added into dilute hydrochloric acid and distilled water, and dispersed evenly by stirring, and then treated in water bath ultrasound until it was flocculent. The PH value of obtained flocculent sample was washed to 7 using water and then dispersed in 20 mL of water.

The 20 mL HF (40%) was added into the container, and 2.0 g of Ti3AlC2 was slowly added while stirring for 10 min. After stirring for 10 min, the obtained suspension was isothermally stirred for 45 h at 25 °C. After stirring, the obtained product was washed to neutral (PH=7), and then the product was vacuum dried for 12 h (60 °C) after high-speed centrifugation to prepare multi-layer Ti3C2Tx material.

0.25 g Ti3C2Tx was added to the obtained NCQDs aqueous dispersion (NCQDs：Ti3C2Tx =2.2:0.25), and continuously stirred for 12 h, followed by standing for 5 h. NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx powders were obtained by high-speed centrifugation and freeze-drying for 48 h. The synthesis process of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx composites is shown in Fig. 1. There are abundant functional groups on the surface of Ti3C2Tx, and small-sized NCQDs are easy to enter the interlayer of Ti3C2Tx. Therefore, the C-O-Ti covalent bonds will be formed by the interaction between the surface functional groups of Ti3C2Tx and NCQDs, so that NCQDs can be rooted on the surface of Ti3C2Tx to form NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx composites.

The morphology and structural information of NCQDs, Ti3C2Tx and NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx were characterized through several test methods. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2F20S-TWIN, USA) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, HITA-CHI, Japan) were used to analyze the morphology of NCQDs, Ti3C2Tx and NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx. The chemical composition and bonds of NCQDs, Ti3C2Tx and NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx were examined using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, NEXUS 670 spectrometer). The crystalline state of NCQDs, Ti3C2Tx and NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx were determined using X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance, Germany) analysis. The morphology change of the wear surface was observed using Scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S-4800, Japan). The chemical property of the worn surface was tested using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Axis Supra, UK). Raman (Renishaw-invia, UK) was used to detect the defect level of NCQDs deposited on the wear surface.

The polyester tribo-film with anti-friction and wear-reduction effect can be easily formed by free fatty acid in SFO on the friction surface, which can expectantly become a green and pollution-free base oil with high-performance, and PAO10, a base oil, was frequently used to explore tribological properties of additives. Therefore, these two oils are used as base oils in this work. The suspensions with different concentrations (0.04–1.5 wt%) were prepared by adding NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx to SFO and PAO10. The dispersion was stirred thoroughly and ultrasonically dispersed for 30 min to realize homogeneous dispersion. The NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx droplets were dropped in the ball-disk gap at room temperature, and the rotating friction test was conducted using the friction and wear tester (Huahui MST-3000). The lubricant performance of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx lubricating additives dispersed in two base oils were conducted. The radius of the steel ball (GCr15) was 2 mm, the size of the rotation steel disc (45# steel) was 20 mm × 3 mm. Balls and disc need to be ultrasonically treated in ethanol before the test, followed by drying to keep the surface clean. The test should be repeated three times.

In the etching process, the Al layer is etched by HF to obtain Ti3C2Tx with interlayer gap. As shown in Fig. 2a, Ti3AlC2, as the precursor material of Ti3C2Tx, has a compact layered structure, while the obtained Ti3C2Tx after HF treatment has obvious interlayer spacing, showing an accordion-like layered structure (Fig. 2b), and stacking degree is greatly reduced. Fig. 2c shows the TEM image of layered Ti3C2Tx. The fair-sized sheet structure is depicted as shown in Fig. 2c, the TEM image of Ti3C2Tx. The TEM images of the NCQDs are shown in Fig. 2d, showing a discrete distribution and a size of less than 10 nm. After the combination between NCQDs and Ti3C2Tx, small-sized NCQDs were uniformly dispersed on the layered structure of Ti3C2Tx (Fig. 2e). As shown in Fig. 2f, NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx composites were synthesized.

As shown in Fig. 3a, the crystal structure of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx was obtained by XRD analysis. After etching, some strong diffraction peaks on the precursor Ti3AlC2 disappeared [21,22], especially the disappearance of the (104) peak indicates the etching of the Al layer [23], and the (002) peak shifting to the left further illustrates the increase of interlayer spacing. Due to the composite of NCQDs, the (002) peak of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx shows an enhanced left shift compared with Ti3C2Tx, which further proves that the introduction of NCQDs can increase the interlayer spacing of Ti3C2Tx [24]. And by calculating the (002) distance between crystal faces of Ti3C2Tx and NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx, the (002) distance increases from 0.92 nm to 1 nm, which can also be consistent with above analysis.

In the analysis of Raman results (Fig. 3b), the characteristic peaks observed at 252, 205, 395 and 624 cm−1 belong to the A1 g and Eg vibration modes [25,26]. In addition, two special peaks were found near 1350 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1, which belong to the typical D-band and G-band of carbon materials [27]. In addition, the NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx composite has a lower ID/IG (0.53) value, indicating a higher degree of graphitization in the composite. It has been reported that Ti3C2Tx with good reducing ability can reduce NCQDs. And during this reduction process, the surface functional groups based on Ti (II or III) may be converted into active sites based on Ti (IV), such as Ti-O groups [28]. The successful composite of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx was further confirmed by structural characterization analysis of Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). In the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 3c), the presence of hydroxyl groups terminated on the surface of the nanosheets was confirmed by a signal peak at 3425 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of the -OH group [29]. The absorption peaks at around 1314 cm−1 and 500–700 cm−1 are ascribed to the -C-N and Ti-O groups of NCQDs and NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx, respectively. The appearance of C-F groups on the spectrum is attributed to the etching of Ti3AlC2 by HF, which confirms the recombination of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx. The dispersion stability of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx was examined as shown in Fig. S7b, after 5 h, NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx precipitates slightly in SFO and PAO10, which can be attributed to the fact that Ti3C2Tx is more inclined to disperse in polar solvents. Compared with the dispersibility of Ti3C2Tx in SFO and PAO10 (Figure. S7b), the dispersibility of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx is improved to some extent, which has certain improvement on the friction performance.

The tribological performance of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx SFO dispersions were comprehensively analyzed under various concentrations, loads and speeds. Fig. 4a showed the friction curves of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx dispersed in SFO with different concentrations. The friction curves using SFO lubrication are significantly higher than those of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx SFO dispersions, and the running-in period of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx SFO dispersions is significantly shorter than that of SFO (Fig. 4a). With the increasement of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx content, the coefficient of friction does not increase or decrease with the increase of lubricant additive content stubbornly. The reason is that the excessive or insufficient of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx lubricant additive in SFO will worsen the lubrication performance. When the additive is insufficient, it is difficult to form a stable lubricating film, so that cannot play an effective lubricating role. Excessive lubricating additives will contribute to adhesion and furrow effect, thus forming agglomeration at the friction surface, so as to damaging the friction surface, which ultimately result in excessive friction coefficient [30]. Therefore, when the addition amount is 0.05 wt% (Fig. 4d), the minimum coefficient of friction was obtained, and mean friction coefficient and wear track width at this concentration are 0.08 and 79.07, respectively. After changing the load, the running-in period is almost unchanged (Fig. 4b illustration). When the load is higher than 2 N, the coefficient of friction starts to go up (Fig. 4b), which may result from the change of the structure of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx under load. As shown in Fig. 4e, the mean friction coefficient and the wear track width also exhibit same trend, and the friction is the smallest at 1.5 N. But the curves suddenly rise at 25 min, such phenomenon demonstrated that friction failure occurred in the process of friction. These results demonstrated that although the lowest friction coefficient was obtained, the tribo-film in unstable. Therefore, the friction conditions need to be further ensured. Therefore, the tribological properties of 0.05 wt% NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx sunflower oil dispersion (1.5 N) were further evaluated at 100–400 rpm. It can be seen from Fig. 4c that with the increase of rotational speed, although the running-in period does not change greatly, the friction coefficient continues to increase with the increase of rotational speed. And the sudden rise in the Fig. 4c occurred at about 10 min, which explain that friction failure occurs in advance at 300 rpm. This is because the shear force changes under the action of rotational speed, contributing to the formation of unstable friction film. As shown in Fig. 4f, the average friction coefficient and wear track width remain the low level (0.04, 56.63 µm) at low speed (100 rpm) lower than those of Ti3C2Tx and NCQDs. The above results show that the NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx lubricant additive significantly improves the comprehensive tribological properties of SFO, and attains the reduction of mean friction coefficient and wear track width by 73% and 71%, respectively.

Fig. 5a shows the friction curve of different concentrations of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx PAO10 dispersions. It can be seen that PAO10 base oil without NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx lubricant additive shows poor lubrication performance, and as shown in Fig. 5d, the mean friction coefficient and wear track width become significantly higher. As shown in Fig. 5a, after the introduction of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx lubricant additive in PAO10 base oil, the friction coefficient showed a significant improvement, the introduction of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx lubricant additive in PAO10 markedly reduced the friction coefficient and the running-in period was shorter than that of PAO10 (Fig. 5a), and with the increasement of concentration, the friction coefficient decreased first and then rose. The concentration affects whether the dispersion can form a steady lubricating film in the friction process. When the concentration is less than 0.075 wt%, the nanoparticles entering the friction gap are not enough to form a long-term stable lubricating film. When the concentration is higher than 0.075 wt%, the dispersion of nanoparticles in the base oil becomes worse, resulting in the deterioration of the lubrication capacity. When the load changes in the range of 0.5–2.5 N, the coefficient of friction reaches the lowest value at 1 N (Fig. 5b), and the mean friction coefficient and the wear track width are also minimized at this time, and then increase with the increase of the load (Fig. 5e), that is, a certain load can play a catalytic role in the tribo-chemical reaction between the nanoparticles and the metal surface to form a complex tribo-chemical protective film, and under relatively high load, the nanoparticles fail to bear high contact stress, as well as enter into the contact interface, thus achieving anti-friction and anti-wear effects [31]. The friction coefficient of 0.075 wt% NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx dispersed into PAO10 at 0.5 N is very close to the minimum value, but the friction curve fluctuates, indicating that the friction process is unstable, so it is slightly higher than the friction coefficient at 1 N. The change of rotate speed contributes to the change of shear force, but at a certain speed, a stable lubricating film can be formed. It could be seen that the friction coefficient associated with the speed change shown in Fig. 5c, and when the speed is 300 rpm, it reaches the smallest value. The lowest average friction coefficient and wear track width were 0.03 and 47.39, which were 75% and 66% (Fig. 5f) lower than those of PAO10, respectively. Moreover, the contact pressure was estimated under different rotation speeds shown in Fig. S5, the results showed that with the increasing of the rotation speed, the contact pressure first go up and then decline, the maximum value at 300 rpm. The results demonstrated that NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx can compensate for the mass loss on the steel disk surface. And a stable lubricating film could be quickly formed by NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx to reduce friction and wear. Compared with Ti3C2Tx and NCQDs, the lubricant situation is also significantly improved (Fig. 5f). It is worth noted that the friction curve of 0.075 wt% NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx dispersed into PAO10 at 1 N and 300 rpm is relatively smooth, which reflects that the complex physical and chemical reactions occurred on the friction interface, forming a protective friction film [32].

The long-term lubrication stability of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx dispersion was evaluated by prolonging the friction time. In the friction experiment for 300 min, the friction coefficient of SFO fluctuated around 0.15, and the friction coefficient of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx SFO dispersion decreased significantly, but the friction curve still fluctuated ( Fig. 6a). This can be attributed to the deterioration of sunflower oil under the friction heat for a long time, forming an unstable friction polyester film, which causes low stability lubrication at the friction interface. After 300 min, the wear track width is only 217.53 µm (Fig. 6b), and the surface has sparse gully (Fig. 6c). The friction coefficient of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx PAO10 dispersion was significantly stable and low (Fig. 6a), and the surface track was shallow and narrow (Fig. 6e), with a width of only 106.97 µm (Fig. 6d). From the results of long-term life analysis, NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx has good anti-friction performance in both base oils.

In order to further clarify the tribological behavior of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx dispersions, the wear track morphology on the steel plate was characterized by optical microscope and SEM. As shown in Fig. 7a, the wear track of SFO lubrication shows dense furrows, and the wear track width is 243.67 µm. The three-dimensional morphology of the wear track (Fig. 7e) shows wide and deep grooves, indicating signs of severe wear. At the same time, a mass of grooves and uneven concave and convex regions on the worn surface are clearly shown on the SEM image (Fig. 7i). However, the wear scar width of 0.05 wt% NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx SFO dispersion was significantly reduced (Fig. 7b). As shown in Fig. 7f, the groove is also relatively shallow. As shown in SEM image (Fig. 7j), it could be seen that the wear surface is relatively flat with slight wear. NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx also showed good lubricity in PAO10. From Fig. 7c, g, it can be seen that the furrows on the wear track of pure PAO10 were dense, and the wear marks were wide and deep. From the SEM image (Fig. 7k) we can see that peeling and abrasive particles were generated around the furrows on the surface, which confirmed that there was negative adhesion and abrasive wear on the friction surface in the process of friction. However, under the lubrication of 0.075 wt% NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx dispersed in PAO10, the adhesion on the steel plate surface almost disappeared, the wear scar width decreased to 47.39 µm (Fig. 7d), and the wear track only existed on the shallow surface of the steel plate (Fig. 7h). As shown in the SEM image of Fig. 7l, the surface becomes flat and smooth, with only a small and minimal peeling. These results indicate that NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx lubricant additives could significantly enhance the tribological properties of SFO and PAO10 and reduce the friction and wear on the surface of steel discs.

The elements on the wear track were analyzed by EDS energy spectrum, and the wear surface showed larger flatness, smaller pits and shallow scratches (Fig. S1a-b). The surface of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx SFO dispersion (Fig. S1 a1-a4) and PAO10 dispersion (Fig. S1 b1-b4) showed a consistent element distribution. C, N, O and Ti elements were uniformly distributed on the worn surface, which provided the possibilities to form anti-wear tribo-film on the friction surface.

The heat and pressure generated in the friction process are easy to cause chemical and physical reactions at the interface. Here, the chemical property on the worn surface lubricated by NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx were analyzed by XPS analysis. As shown in Fig. 8a-b, it is the fine spectrum of N 1 s, O 1 s and Ti 2p on the worn surface of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx SFO dispersion. The fine spectrum of N 1 s (Fig. 8a) shows three characteristic peaks at 398.5, 399.7 and 400.7 eV, which corresponds to C-N, N-H and nitrides. It can be proved that a series of chemical reactions occur in the process of friction, and a protective nitride film is formed [33]. The fine spectrum of O 1 s is shown in Fig. 8b, a peak at 531.6 eV is not only assigned to CO bond, but also to C-O-Ti bond, which confirms the combination between NCQDs and Ti3C2Tx. The peak at 529.6 eV comes from Fe-O/Ti-O, that is, the friction surface may be covered with metal oxides. A friction transfer film was formed by metal oxides and nitrides on the friction surface [34]. The fine spectrum of Ti 2p is shown in Fig. 8c. The peaks locating at 456.2, 459.3, 461.7 and 463.1 eV correspond to Ti-O (Ti 2p1/2), Ti-O (Ti 2p1/2), Ti-C (Ti 2p1/2) and Ti-C (Ti 2p3/2). The Ti-O bond at 456.2 and 459.3 eV corresponds to the peak at 529.6 eV of the O 1 s fine spectrum [35]. As shown in Fig. 8d-f, it is the XPS spectrum of the worn surface of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx PAO10 dispersion. Three peaks appearing in the fine spectrum of N 1 s confirm the existence of nitrides. Peaks at 529.6, 456.2 and 459 eV (Fig. 8e, f) of O 1 s and Ti 2p fine spectra can be attributed to the metal oxide. Therefore, the chemical friction reactions can be induced by friction heat and shear force generated in the sliding surface, and the severe friction and wear is avoided, which results from the formation of friction transfer film made up of nitrides and metal oxides.

The FT-IR spectra of the worn surface are shown in Fig. 9a. It can be seen that the chemical groups of the NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx dispersion on the worn surface are consistent with the NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx, which further confirms that NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx plays a lubricating role on the friction surface. The Raman spectra of the wear track (Fig. 9b) show four peaks in the range of 151–624 cm−1, which can also be observed in the Raman spectra of Ti3C2Tx. Therefore, it is verified that the reduction of friction and wear is attributed to the induction of a dense friction film composed of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx nanosheets, which is easy to shear and reduces the possibility of metal-to-metal contact. The peaks locating at 1368 and 1588 cm−1 are typical D (disordered) and G (graphite) peaks of carbon materials, so the friction time is prolonged, and the change of ID/IG value with time is analyzed. As shown in Fig. 9c, the friction surface ID/IG values of the two dispersions of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx increase slightly with time, that is, the degree of disorder increases. NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx nanoparticles entered the friction gap, and the stress caused structural damage, indicating that NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx played a role in bearing friction. In addition, the structure is destroyed for incompletely graphitized NCQDs, which may explain that the structure of NCQDs gradually changes from disorder to order, forming an ordered graphite-like tribo-layer, which benefits friction [36].

Based on the above discussion, NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx significantly enhanced the lubricant performances of SFO and PAO10, but lubrication state during the friction process needs further calculation. The lubrication state is determined by the ratio of the minimum oil film thickness (h0) to the roughness at the wear point (σ) [37]. The calculation formula of the minimum oil film thickness is as follows [38]:
(1)
h
0
= 2.69
G *
0.53
U *
0.67
R
W *
0.067
1 - 0.61
e
- 0.73 k
Where W* =W/E1R2, G* =αE1, U* =ηu/E1R. Since the concentration of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx dispersed in the oil is small, the pressure-viscosity coefficient (SFO α1 =11.9 GPa−1 [39], PAO10 α2 =22 GPa−1 [40]) of SFO and water is used to estimate h0. η is regarded as the dynamic viscosity of lubricant (η1 =2.91 ×10−2 Pa·s [41] and η2 =5.7 ×10−2 Pa·s [42]); u is the velocity of the friction pair (62.8 mm/s); The radius of the sphere is expressed as R (2 mm); w is the load in the experiment process (W1 =3 N, W2 =1 N); k is a coefficient (about 1); E1 = 233 GPa [43].
(2)
λ =
h
0
σ
1
2
+
σ
2
2

Therefore, the least film thicknesses of SFO suspension (0.05 wt%) and PAO10 suspension (0.075 wt%) were calculated to be 34.64 nm and 79.96 nm, respectively. From Fig. S2a-b, it can be seen that surface roughness (σ) of ball and disk lubricated by 0.05 wt% NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx SFO dispersion is σ1 = 6.94 nm and σ2 = 9.47 nm respectively. As shown in Fig. S2c-d, the surface roughness of the sphere and disk of 0.075 wt% NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx PAO10 dispersion are σ1 = 12.31 nm and σ2 = 13.76 nm, respectively. Therefore, according to the formula (2), the λ value of 0.05 wt% NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx SFO dispersion is about 2.9 (1 <2.9 < 3), and the λ value of 0.075 wt% NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx PAO10 dispersion is about 4.3 (>3), that is, under the lubrication of these two dispersions, they are mixed lubrication regime and fluid lubrication regime respectively.

On the basis of the relevant evaluation and analysis of tribological properties, the tribological mechanism of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx lubricating additive in the friction process is discussed here. As shown in Fig. 10a, the friction process is a ball-on-disk rotating friction process. The micro-area diagram of the contact between the ball and the disc is shown in Fig. 10b.The NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx dispersion enters the friction gap, and under the action of friction heat and pressure, tribo-chemical reactions are easily induced to form a tribo-transfer film composed of nitrides and metal oxides, avoiding direct contact between the friction pairs [44]. Secondly, under pressure and shear force, sliding occurring between Ti3C2Tx layers bears part of the friction and transforms into internal friction between layers [45] (Fig. 10c). In particular, small-sized NCQDs nanoparticles can act as rolling bearings during friction (Fig. 10c). Therefore, the conjunctive use of layered structure and spherical nanoparticles can show synergistic lubrication properties to lower friction and wear [46]. With the extension of friction working time, NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx is easily destroyed in the friction system, forming smaller fragments and compensating for the depression of the friction surface, which can smooth out the friction surface and reduce the friction force (Fig. 10d). This is because the metal surface has a large number of positive charges during the friction process, which makes it easy for the O and N atoms on the NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx composite to attract Fe atoms through hydrogen bonds and van der Waals interactions, which helps to enhance the filling adsorption strength of the additive and the metal surface [47,48]. Therefore, NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx forms a friction film during the friction process, which cooperates with lubrication and filling, and helps to realize the anti-friction and wear-reduction at the friction interface.

In this work, NCQDs were assembled onto the layered structure of Ti3C2Tx by using the affinity of the active groups on the surface of Ti3C2Tx, so as to realize the synergistic lubrication of zero-dimensional materials and two-dimensional materials and further promote the tribological performance of additives. The tribological properties of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx were evaluated in base oils SFO and PAO10. The results show that NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx dispersed in base oil can exhibit remarkable friction-reduction and anti-wear performance. These improved results are mainly attributed to the chemical reaction of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx took place on the friction surface, thus forming a complex friction transfer film, which avoid direct contact between the friction surfaces. In addition, the NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx composite plays a synergistic effect combining ball bearing and layer-to-layer sliding, and NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx fragments filled at the surface depression improves the surface smoothness and reduces friction and wear. These findings will provide more new ideas for the further application of NCQDs@Ti3C2Tx.

Ding Wang: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Xiaohua Jia: Project administration, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yiping Zhang: Data curation. Zhiqiang Shan: Formal analysis. Jin Yang: Software. Yunfeng Su: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Haojie Song: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
